title: Toad in the hole
subtitle: Yorkshire pudding batter baked around pork sausages
excerpt: Pork sausages half-buried in a tall, crisp Yorkshire pudding batter, baked in a single roasting tin. A weeknight supper and a Sunday staple.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Sausage and batter pudding entries.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Batter pudding methods.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature before using.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving a batter to stand after mixing so the starch granules fully hydrate and the gluten relaxes, giving a lighter, more even rise.

content.text: Toad in the hole is the same batter as Yorkshire pudding poured around sausages in a single large tin. The sausages sit half-buried in batter that puffs up around them, golden and crisp at the edges, soft and custardy underneath. It is one of the great cheap dinners.
The rules are the same as for Yorkshire puddings: smoking-hot fat, cold batter, oven door shut for the first 25 minutes.
A 30 x 20 cm roasting tin is the right size for 8 sausages. The tin needs to be deep enough (at least 4 cm) for the batter to puff.

Whisk the {{plain-flour}} of flour and salt in a large bowl. Make a well, add the {{eggs}} eggs, and whisk to a smooth paste. Gradually whisk in the {{whole-milk}} of milk until the batter is thin and smooth.
for at least 30 minutes at room temperature.
Roast the sausages, then add the batter
Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Put the fat into the roasting tin and heat in the oven for 5 minutes until smoking. Add the sausages and roast for 15 minutes, turning once, until lightly browned.
Working quickly, pour the cold batter around and over the sausages. Return to the oven immediately and close the door.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until puffed, deeply golden, and set. Do not open the oven before 25 minutes.

items.cause: The fat was not hot enough, or the oven door was opened too soon.
items.fix: The fat must be smoking when the batter goes in. Do not open the door for the first 25 minutes.

items.symptom: The batter is doughy in the middle
items.cause: Underbaked, or the tin was too deep and narrow.
items.fix: Use a wide, shallow tin so the batter spreads and cooks through. Bake for the full 30 minutes.

items.symptom: Sausages are raw inside when the batter is done
items.cause: The sausages were not pre-cooked before the batter went in.
items.fix: Roast the sausages for a full 15 minutes before adding the batter so they are mostly cooked through.

items.cause: The tin was too cold when the batter went in, or the fat was insufficient.
items.fix: Make sure the tin is smoking hot. There needs to be enough fat to cover the entire base, about 2 to 3 mm deep.

content.text: Red onion, thinly sliced and caramelised, scattered over the batter just before it goes in the oven gives a sweet, jammy contrast to the savoury batter. Mustard-and-herb sausages give a more aromatic result.

Toad in the hole appears in British cookbooks from the late eighteenth century, originally made with cheaper cuts of meat or leftover roast. The sausage version became standard in the twentieth century as pork sausages became an everyday purchase. It remains one of the most-searched British recipes, partly for its name and partly because it is deeply satisfying to make and eat.
